edges.description: Loss of PANK2 activity reduces cellular CoA availability.
CoA depletion compromises mitochondrial metabolism and energy homeostasis.
Mitochondrial metabolic failure reduces phosphopantetheinyl-protein abundance.
Reduced phosphopantetheinyl proteins impair lipoylation and pyruvate dehydrogenase activity.
Mitochondrial dysfunction and impaired iron-sulfur homeostasis culminate in preferential iron deposition in the basal ganglia.
Basal ganglia dysfunction manifests clinically as progressive dystonia.
Basal ganglia dysfunction contributes to speech impairment.
Motor pathway involvement contributes to spasticity.
Progressive extrapyramidal dysfunction contributes to swallowing difficulty.
Progressive neurodegeneration can also affect cognition and behavior.
Neuroretinal vulnerability contributes to retinal degeneration.
Early-onset PKAN can present with developmental delay before motor regression becomes prominent.

nodes.description: Iron preferentially accumulates in the globus pallidus and basal ganglia, producing the classic eye-of-the-tiger imaging phenotype and contributing to neurodegeneration.

evidence.reference_title: Pathology and treatment methods in pantothenate kinase-associated neurodegeneration.
evidence.snippet: The condition is caused by a mutation in the PANK2 gene, which results in iron accumulation in the brain

evidence.reference_title: Natural history and genotype‐phenotype correlation of pantothenate kinase‐associated neurodegeneration.
evidence.snippet: The typical manifestation of neuroimaging is the “eye-of-the-tiger” sign

evidence.reference_title: Natural history and genotype‐phenotype correlation of pantothenate kinase‐associated neurodegeneration.
evidence.snippet: The mutations result in a decreased activity of pantothenic acid kinase 2, which is a key regulatory enzyme in coenzyme A production by pantothenic acid, possibly leading to the reduction of coenzyme A3 and accumulation of its substrates, cysteine, which may chelate iron accumulation.

nodes.description: Reduced CoA production is an early biochemical consequence of PANK2 loss.

evidence.reference_title: Natural history and genotype-phenotype correlation of pantothenate kinase-associated neurodegeneration.
evidence.snippet: The mutations result in a decreased activity of pantothenic acid kinase 2,

evidence.reference_title: Pathology and treatment methods in pantothenate kinase-associated neurodegeneration.
evidence.snippet: The condition is caused by a mutation in the PANK2 gene, which results in iron accumulation in the brain and changes in the functioning of biochemical pathways dependent on coenzyme A.

nodes.description: Cognitive decline occurs in a substantial subset of patients, especially with early-onset disease.

evidence.reference_title: Natural history and genotype‐phenotype correlation of pantothenate kinase‐associated neurodegeneration.
evidence.snippet: About 54.0% (34/63) in early‐onset group and 33.8% (24/71) in late‐onset group were reported to show cognitive decline after disease onset.

nodes.description: Speech impairment is common in PKAN, affecting patients with both classic and atypical forms.

evidence.reference_title: Natural history and genotype‐phenotype correlation of pantothenate kinase‐associated neurodegeneration.
evidence.snippet: Dysarthria occurred in 86.4% (89/103) of early‐onset and 80.0% (80/100) of late‐onset patients.

nodes.description: Bulbar dysfunction can produce swallowing difficulty in PKAN.

evidence.snippet: Patients with PKAN exhibit a variety of symptoms, including dystonia, rigidity, bradykinesia, spasticity, difficulty swallowing and speaking, shortened lifespan, and sometimes cognitive and visual impairment.

nodes.description: Progressive dystonia is the core movement disorder in PKAN.

evidence.reference_title: Natural history and genotype‐phenotype correlation of pantothenate kinase‐associated neurodegeneration.
evidence.snippet: Dystonia in lower limbs was the most common initial symptom in both early- and late-onset groups.

nodes.description: Early-onset PKAN may present with developmental delay before overt motor regression.

evidence.reference_title: Natural history and genotype‐phenotype correlation of pantothenate kinase‐associated neurodegeneration.
evidence.snippet: Developmental delay (DD) was more common in early‐onset patients before disease onset.

nodes.description: Reduced lipoylation compromises pyruvate dehydrogenase activity and redox homeostasis.

evidence.reference_title: Down regulation of the expression of mitochondrial phosphopantetheinyl-proteins in pantothenate kinase-associated neurodegeneration: pathophysiological consequences and therapeutic perspectives
evidence.snippet: PDH lipoylation and activity were severely reduced in PKAN cells.

nodes.description: CoA shortage compromises mitochondrial metabolism and energy homeostasis.

evidence.reference_title: Pathology and treatment methods in pantothenate kinase-associated neurodegeneration.
evidence.snippet: The condition is caused by a mutation in the PANK2 gene, which results in iron accumulation in the brain and changes in the functioning of biochemical pathways dependent on coenzyme A.

nodes.description: Loss of mitochondrial phosphopantetheinylated proteins disrupts lipoylation and pyruvate dehydrogenase function.

evidence.reference_title: Down regulation of the expression of mitochondrial phosphopantetheinyl-proteins in pantothenate kinase-associated neurodegeneration: pathophysiological consequences and therapeutic perspectives
evidence.snippet: the expression levels of 4′-phosphopantetheinyl proteins such as mtACP, ALDH1L2 and AASS were dramatically reduced

evidence.reference_title: Natural history and genotype-phenotype correlation of pantothenate kinase-associated neurodegeneration.
evidence.snippet: Patients who met all the following criteria were enrolled: (a) dystonia; (b) “eye‐of‐the‐tiger” sign on brain MRI; and (c) biallelic pathogenic or likely pathogenic variants in PANK2.

nodes.description: Pathogenic PANK2 variants impair the mitochondrial pantothenate kinase 2 enzyme and initiate the downstream neurodegenerative cascade.

evidence.reference_title: Altered neuronal mitochondrial coenzyme A synthesis in neurodegeneration with brain iron accumulation caused by abnormal processing, stability, and catalytic activity of mutant pantothenate kinase 2.
evidence.snippet: PanK2 protein is localized to mitochondria of neurons in human brain, distinguishing it from other pantothenate kinases that do not possess mitochondrial-targeting sequences.

evidence.reference_title: Altered neuronal mitochondrial coenzyme A synthesis in neurodegeneration with brain iron accumulation caused by abnormal processing, stability, and catalytic activity of mutant pantothenate kinase 2.
evidence.snippet: The mature protein catalyzes the initial step in coenzyme A (CoA) synthesis

nodes.description: Retinal degeneration or optic nerve atrophy can accompany the neurologic syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Down regulation of the expression of mitochondrial phosphopantetheinyl-proteins in pantothenate kinase-associated neurodegeneration: pathophysiological consequences and therapeutic perspectives
evidence.snippet: muscle rigidity, neuropsychiatric symptoms, and retinal degeneration or optic nerve atrophy.

nodes.description: Upper motor neuron features can accompany the dystonia-predominant syndrome.

evidence.snippet: Patients with PKAN exhibit a variety of symptoms, including dystonia, rigidity, bradykinesia, spasticity, difficulty swallowing and speaking, shortened lifespan, and sometimes cognitive and visual impairment.

nodes.description: Visual loss occurs in some patients and may be an early presenting symptom.

evidence.reference_title: Natural history and genotype‐phenotype correlation of pantothenate kinase‐associated neurodegeneration.
evidence.snippet: Some patients initially presented with vision loss, generalized dystonia, involuntary movement, or cognitive impairment.
